output: Thank you, Mr. President. I welcome the approach outlined in the White Paper, which aims to identify the root causes of malnutrition and obesity, and the resulting diseases.
Unfortunately, in food policy in Europe, regardless of whether we are discussing New Proffi, food labelling, or certain aspects in the White Paper, we lapse into a very one-sided approach.
We are indeed attempting to solve the problems we have in Europe through product policy.
I believe it is fundamentally flawed policy to assume that there are good and bad foods.
Amendment proposals show that this does not exist.
There are only good and bad, or likewise balanced and unbalanced, diets.
should be our approach.
Causes of incorrect nutrition are multifaceted
We will not achieve our goal by attempting to influence consumers through product labeling, or by imposing advertising bans or regulations, or by applying different VAT rates.

I believe everything we are doing here does not intend to pre-empt the labelling regulation debate, but everything we are doing here should fall under the heading: what can we do to encourage consumers to reflect on their eating habits?
A traffic light label suggesting that I am currently facing a good or bad product strikes me as an instrument of manipulation rather than one of education and enlightenment.
Therefore, I would like to see a balanced approach here and the recognition that relying solely on product policy will not advance us further. Thank you, Senior Krammer, for your time.
Mr. President, Madam Vice-President, dear colleagues! The Committee on Budgetary Control has no new topics.
We are satisfied with the old issues and would also be satisfied with progress on the old topics.
Five points first of all: our urgent request for support for Romania and Bulgaria in building the rule of law, the judiciary, and financial oversight.
We do not want to be in the same position in two years' time as we are today. That is why this point is extremely important to us.
The second point: the reliability statement for the overall budget pursuant to the tiered control procedure, including the national audit institutions.
It would be desirable if, in the final six months during which the Commission remains in office, we could achieve decisive progress here, or at least agree on a common path.
One is expected to be modest as a household auditor.
The third point would be simplification.
We must provide greater support to the lower levels of administration in Member States in implementing EU law.
It becomes apparent that they are entirely overwhelmed by large parts of EU law. This is why the error rates are so high.
fourth point: external assistance—here, I would particularly like to address Kosovo and the issue of cooperation with the United Nations
The budgetary control committee has been to Kosovo, and I would like to announce now that Kosovo will play a certain role in the discharge procedure.
I believe we have so far remained far below the potential of the European Union, and that we urgently need to catch up here.
The fifth point would be improving cooperation among the member states in combating fraud.
We have homework here, primarily for the ladies and gentlemen of the council.
The Parliament will vote on the legal basis—the regulation on combating fraud—in November. Then we will need to jointly consider how we can proceed if we wish to achieve better results in this field.
was, at the very least, our shared wish until now
Mr. President! India and Europe need more and better contacts with each other at all levels, particularly at the level of so-called ordinary people.
business travelers, tourists, students, and so on—a brief note on this: it also has significance for the Commission's legislative programme, which we discussed previously.
In global air traffic, we need effective safety regulations—ones that actually make a difference—not merely symbolic measures.
It would therefore be a sensible, truly necessary measure to finally abolish the senseless liquid regulations once and for all and come up with something better.
I voted in favor of the resolution because it sends a clear message to the Belarusian government to finally respect human rights.

It is disappointing that the hopes for a democratic development for the people of this last European dictatorship were not fulfilled by the questionable, indeed falsified, parliamentary elections of September.
The same applies to the strategic suppression of the opposition and civil society.
At the same time, today we have called on the Council and the Commission to facilitate and strengthen contacts between peoples and to democratize the country by seriously exploring the possibility of
Reducing visa fees for Belarusian citizens when entering the Schengen Area, thereby preventing the growing isolation of Belarus and its citizens.
I would like to first protest against the spirit of a statement heard just a few minutes ago, according to which foreigners would flood our labor market.
Secondly, I would have preferred a clearer, more explicit report from the employment committee.
Trust in the social cohesive power of the European Union can only be achieved if fundamental social rights are defined as primary European law.

We should send a stronger signal to the Council, the Commission, the Member States, and the ECJ, and not be content with merely demanding a balance between fundamental rights and internal market freedoms and free movement.
We are not changing this. Social rights, like civil liberties and human rights, must not be restricted by the free movement provisions of the internal market.
The issue is that we must defend and improve the European social model, and it is high time to introduce a social progress clause as a binding protocol to the existing EU treaties.
It is time to amend the Posting of Workers Directive in such a way that pay and minimum standards requirements may no longer be limited to minimum requirements. Thank you.
Mr. President! I would like to join the many colleagues who have already rightly expressed their thanks to the rapporteur, Doris Ba.
We have someone with her who, hopefully, does not actually forget the lifeblood, but has invested a great deal of lifeblood in this space.
I am pleased with you and the colleagues who have also said this, that some progress has been made, also in the field of police cooperation.
This is important in order for everyday life on the ground to function properly.

I still believe, however, that we must continually remind our counterparts in Bosnia and Herzegovina that they themselves must contribute considerably more, indeed more than is currently being done in certain areas.
Internal cooperation, and the willingness to engage in this internal work within the ethnic group, is something we must continually demand.
It's not the case that everyone tries to go their own way.
Moreover, integration into regional cooperation in the Western Balkans is important.
Madam President! The distance between the European Union and its citizens must not become as great as it has become.
We must ensure that it is reduced.
In this context, the ombudsman has an important role, particularly when, where, and wherever he performs it well.
It is especially important that we can count on the ombudsman's commitment and good judgment.
The issue is that we should not raise expectations that no one can fulfill.

But it is also about ensuring that wherever shortcomings have arisen within the administration of European institutions, they are addressed and, where possible, reasonable solutions are sought here that can then reliably guarantee and satisfy the citizen.
The previous practice has shown that the Ombudsman takes on this task seriously and performs it well.
We may hope that this will continue to be the case. We thank you and the rapporteur for this report.
Colleagues, nine years after the realization of the monetary union, Europe is facing a serious crisis.
Banks collapse or are propped up with billions from the state budget. Sales collapse, and millions of people fear for their jobs and their future.
But not only has the market failed, the prevailing politics also seem incapable of learning from its mistakes.
We also believe that making such intervening remarks does not particularly distinguish you. We hold the view that serious errors were made in the design of the Economic and Monetary Union.
Such a mistake is the structural disintegration of money and fiscal policy. One cannot create a common currency without simultaneously at least partially harmonizing tax and expenditure policy.
I believe the economic imbalances within the eurozone have greatly intensified.
What we actually need now is better coordination of economic and, above all, tax policy.
We need effective measures against tax dumping; furthermore, tax havens must be closed down, and capital flows must finally be brought under control again.
The second major flaw lies in the design of the Stability and Growth Pact

Anyone who considers fiscal consolidation unavoidable in times like these evidently lives in a different world. Nothing would be more disastrous than responding to this economic crisis now with public spending cuts.
The Stability Pact has clearly demonstrated its failure.
It should be replaced by an integrated European strategy for solidarity and sustainable development.
In our view, what is needed is an investment offensive to renew public infrastructure and improve the situation of socially disadvantaged people in Europe.
A third error lies in the structure of the European Central Bank itself, which is not subject to democratic oversight and is solely committed to the goal of price stability.

We advocate for democratic oversight of the European Central Bank, and furthermore call for a revision of the ECB's monetary policy mandate, requiring that in the future growth and employment be promoted on an equal footing with price stability.
The current crisis also presents an opportunity for profound reforms of the European monetary and financial architecture.
This opportunity should not be recklessly squandered.
Madam President, Commissioner, President of the Council, esteemed colleagues! Indeed, the automotive industry is in crisis, and there are many reasons for this.
In particular, the financial crisis has further intensified the crisis in this sector, and currently, efforts are underway in the member states to develop solutions.
The European automotive industry is a key industry.
And therefore, we also need European solutions for this key European industry in order to overcome the crisis.
Almost all automobile manufacturers have European structures.

To make the necessary investments in environmentally friendly technologies, which have been mentioned several times today, automobile manufacturers require public guarantees or low-interest loans from the European Investment Bank.
But the supplier industry, which would be very important, must not be overlooked in this context.
Especially in the supplier industry, it is small and medium-sized enterprises that create jobs and where innovations take place.
But what can the European Union do to protect this key industry?
At the previous plenary session, the Council President made a proposal to protect key industries.
A good suggestion, as I see it, is that if one consistently carries this idea through to its conclusion, it would mean a Volkswagen Act for the entire European automotive industry.
That would certainly be a useful tool.
However, this protective instrument is currently being challenged by the European Commission.
Given my question to the Commission: Will the European Commission stick to its litigation despite the crisis in the automotive industry, or will it now change course in light of the crisis?
Dear Mr. President, Mr. President of the European Court of Auditors, Vice-President of the European Commission, dear colleagues! Indeed, it has now become customary to observe.

We must acknowledge that, in the relationship between expenditures at the European level and administrative activities at the national level, a declaration of reliability cannot be provided once again.

I recall conversations with the former President of the Court of Auditors, Professor Friedmann, who once told me that, given the structures of God, it is absolutely impossible for him to provide a statement of assurance.
Therefore, we should already consider together how this instrument can be imbued with life in such a way that the objective of a reliability declaration can be achieved when justified.
And here we must naturally distinguish a few things from one another.
First, a budget that includes approximately 95% subsidies is far more susceptible to fraud than a national, regional, or local budget.

A subsidy budget, which is largely managed by the member states, who have demanded significantly greater autonomy from us in the new financial period, as the criticism during the previous funding period had been that too much was being centrally controlled.

These are also things that are always gladly thrown together.
I also get annoyed when EU funds are used to support things that are not absolutely necessary.
But that was not fraud; it was wastefulness.

Therefore, those responsible for the projects—particularly in the field of structural policy—have the Member States, as well as the responsibility to ensure that funds are not wasted and that only projects receiving support are those that truly generate added value for the region.
Therefore, we should certainly consider, on occasion, shifting parts of structural policy toward interest-free loans.
If you have to repay member states, you only support what you actually need. Many thanks.
It is not just me who finds it difficult to join in the growing self-praise of European decision-makers when it comes to managing the financial market crisis.
because if Europe were this EU a functioning democracy, one would first have to raise the question of responsibility and how all this came to be
It is simply not true that all the problems we face are like a tsunami and naturally inevitable, but rather are created by human action.
And given the extensive regulatory framework that this Union has established, one must wonder: Why was no action taken when banks began crossing national borders on a significant scale?
Why was nothing done despite appropriate warnings, as derivatives had so insidiously crept in?
I clearly recall that Nobel laureate Joseph Stiglitz addressed all these points.
There was silence, there was a taboo.
One would have to start there, because recognizing one's own mistakes is the only key to finding solutions for the future.

Do you believe this could solve the problem of these two countries?
The second question is: Do you believe it would be possible to introduce a risk premium for derivatives, which would then be used to strengthen bank equity?

wouldn't it be more reasonable to consider tax reductions for our businesses and our employees, to pay investment allowances, and to accordingly adjust the progressive depreciation?

Madam President! The comprehensive report illustrates how many human rights violations occur daily around the world.
This is particularly alarming 60 years after the proclamation of the Charter of Human Rights and calls upon us to intensify our efforts even further.
For this reason as well, I would like to advocate for establishing the current Subcommittee on Human Rights as a main committee.
Today, we have awarded the Sacharow Prize to Hojia, who advocates for human rights and the environment in China.
Because he is incarcerated, he was unable to take him over in person.
Shame on China! And tomorrow, a debate on human rights violations in Russia will take place here.
In our relations with these two countries, we must not only give economic interests due consideration, but above all ensure that human rights are accorded a higher priority.
We demand freedom for Hodjia in China and freedom for Mikhail Khodorkovsky in Russia.

Mr. President, esteemed members of the Council, Commissioner, dear colleagues! As rapporteur for the Anti-Fraud Office, one makes interesting observations—such as the fact that everyone talks about combating fraud, yet nobody actually does anything.
Or the experience of member states meticulously calculating their payments to the European Union with a fine stylus, yet putting on extravagant spending habits when it comes to disbursing this money.
and even make it easier for fraudsters, for example, by the fact that it still takes an average of 36 months before irregularities are reported to the European Union at all.
The European Parliament does not agree with this.
We aim for an efficient fraud prevention strategy that also includes prevention.
We want the member states to make combating fraud a priority and for us to discuss jointly the necessities and challenges involved.
We aim to increase national judicial authorities' interest in further pursuing OLA mediation outcomes.
At Grumenturen, there must no longer be areas free of legal regulation as there have been up to now.
We need to achieve parity between European money and national money.
We want an independent authority that, in fulfilling its duties, has the necessary resources and legal basis.
We want an office that can cooperate silently and efficiently with the member states.
Our response to legal basis 17399 covers all these points.
We advocate further developing this regulation as the core of the office for fraud prevention together with the Council.
We request that the Council incorporate our comments. We request that the Council initiate discussions on the further development of this regulation.
We intend to bring the issues concerning OLAF to a resolution.
I thank all the factions of the House that you also support this in a resolution we will vote on tomorrow.
I appeal to the French and Czech Presidencies of the Council to pick up the ball thrown by Parliament and pass it back.
We have also taken up solutions in our proposals that were developed and submitted by the Council's working group.
On our part, I would like to express our interest in a swift conclusion of this regulation and assure you of our constructive willingness to discuss it based on our proposals.
The consolidation of all OLAF legal bases, as the Council has envisaged, would entail an additional substantial loss of time with an uncertain outcome.
Ola is celebrating his tenth birthday soon.
A consolidation means another ten years without a improved legal basis.
We take the fight against fraud too seriously for that.
Therefore, we want to achieve what is feasible now, in cooperation with the Council.

Mr. President! I would like to pick up on a remark made earlier by my Austrian colleague, Paul Rübig.

We spend less than 1 percent of the European gross domestic product on Europe, while simultaneously demanding that the European Union take on ever more tasks for an ever-growing number of member states.
This account cannot add up. It is therefore genuinely unbelievable that for many years we have pursued a savings policy, yet this savings policy is not reflected in frugality
not to use our funds, but essentially to enable finance ministers to collect money again by the end of the year and have it flow into their national budgets.
We would have enough new tasks for which we should spend our money.
We also have tasks that we have not adequately funded financially for years.
I believe the EU's information budgets should be significantly increased if we truly wish to connect European citizens with Europe.
There are numerous tasks in the field of student and apprentice exchange where Europe could play a significant role.
It also advances a traditionally state-reserved domain within the justice sector.
This reflects the changed living conditions of people in our Union.
But and this remark has already been made several times: integration must not extend too far into traditionally established and especially into functioning structures.
Not everything may be swept under the rug.
Public documents and public officials authorized to issue them are structured quite differently in many member states.
In Austria, but not only here, the notary public has long been established as a public document authority and enjoys a high level of security and trust.
This must not be jeopardized unnecessarily, simply because such occupational groups do not exist elsewhere.
Therefore, integration and mutual openness, yes, but with moderation.
Thank you very much, Madam President, esteemed colleagues! Plant protection products have been and remain essential tools in agricultural production.
Farmers require a minimum selection of plant protection products, as this concerns the protection of the domestic cultural landscape, high-quality nutrition, and the preservation of resources.
We often conduct discussions about chemicals on a highly irrational level.
But to achieve reasonable results, we must think and act rationally.
The consequences of the original parliamentary position would have been fatal: a broad ban on plant protection products, resulting in lower agricultural yields and, ultimately, rising food prices.
Therefore, I am glad that a barely acceptable compromise was reached in the trilogue negotiations.
It is welcome that we have retained the three-zone regulations, albeit with significant exceptions for the member states.

A courageous step toward a consistent internal market solution looks different at this juncture. In addition to acceptable compromises, we should not anticipate a final definition of endocrine-disrupting substances, but instead genuinely allow the European Commission the four years to present a scientifically sound definition.
Otherwise, we would overstretch the precautionary principle at this point.
Lastly, I regret that we do not proceed to conduct an impact assessment regarding the current agreement. Thank you.
Mr. President, the last time there was a corresponding debate on the visit of a head of government or head of state, you intervened decisively.
At that time, it concerned an issue that was personally important to him, namely the Lisbon Treaty.

If today the president, the democratically elected president of a country, speaks and is severely interrupted and disturbed by representatives from the socialist side, the president does not intervene.
Do you know what this is?
This is not democracy; it is arbitrariness, and I demand that appropriate measures be taken.
It is rare for the interests of citizens and politicians to differ so greatly.
Across Europe, the issue of children ranks highest among citizens.
For politicians, children's issues rank far down on the list of priorities, as we can again observe here.
I know of no country that has a dedicated children's ministry. Likewise, within the Commission there is no specific representative for the interests of our youngest citizens.
In parliament, the issue of children is scattered across all committees.
Therefore, I would like to particularly highlight at this point that the Commission is currently addressing childcare facilities and matters concerning Barcelona.
! In our parliamentary working group on the Quality of Childhood, we have established the following: Our children live in a very complex world.
The future of a person who is a child here and now is completely uncertain.
A child today can climb all the way to the top of the social ladder or plummet all the way to the bottom.
It can remain within its own cultural circle or seek a new one.
It may follow a similar life path as its parents or do exactly the opposite.
One may marry someone of the other or one's own gender.
All of this was more than 50 years ago, when we brought the Union of Europe into being, was entirely different.
The diversity that characterizes us Europeans—this diversity has become a defining factor in the lives of children.
The concept of diversity must therefore be integrated into the pedagogy of childcare workers and teachers.
This is currently taken into account far too little.
European childcare facilities and schools are struggling with the challenge of integration.
In matters of integration, we must press the reset button and go back to the beginning.
Let us approach this topic once again entirely without prejudice, in keeping with the motto Unity through Diversity.

In addition, I urge all European parliamentarians following me not only to support gender mainstreaming, but also to advocate for child mainstreaming—especially now, during the global financial crisis, because otherwise we will burden our children with immense debts. Thank you very much!

I would like to sincerely thank you for these constructive contributions to the debate that I have heard here.

and the second thing I would like to say, which is now directed at my British friends, and that is cross all the parties

for the completely irrational resistance, politically unjustified from both a competition and environmental standpoint, to abandoning minimum standards.
I believe that at this point lies the opportunity to reach a compromise, which I hope will ultimately be achieved during the second reading.
Incidentally, I do not believe we should refrain from seeking a compromise in the first reading.
This legislation is too complex for that purpose.
I do not wish to conclude this legislative term as rapporteur and leave the next Parliament with legislation whose text is inconsistent, illogical, and contradictory.
Therefore, I kindly ask for your support of the compromises when we vote here in one hour. Thank you very much.
Sir, dear colleagues! I believe the crucial question regarding all the economic stimulus programs now being assembled across Europe is naturally who ultimately receives the money?
We will continue issuing blank cheques to the banks, even though ultimate nationalisation would ultimately be significantly cheaper for the taxpayer.
And should corporations and high-income earners continue to receive further relief, despite having been lavishly subsidized through tax giveaways across Europe for years?

The more money is squandered on such purposes, the more inevitable it becomes that these programs will fail, and the more likely it is that the European economy will sink further and further into a very dangerous downward spiral.

The years-long policy of privatization, deregulation, and liberalization—precisely this policy—has increasingly concentrated incomes among the top ten thousand, and it is this policy that is responsible for the current crisis in which we find ourselves today.
Who believes he can overcome this crisis by simply continuing exactly this policy with minor adjustments? I think he has simply understood nothing at all.
The exact opposite of this is required.
Instead of purchasing bad paper from banks, public funds should be used for the renovation of schools and hospitals, and for the ecological transformation of the European economy.

Where private companies receive public funds, it must be stipulated that public money may not be provided without employment guarantees, and above all, without the acquisition of public ownership rights, so that the state, and in particular the general public, can also participate in future profits.
The best possible economic stimulus program currently available would be a radical redistribution of income and wealth from the top down.
The informal sector in Europe must be curbed, rather than continually promoted.
We need higher minimum wages and better social benefits in Europe.

And we need taxes that ensure that millionaires and the beneficiaries of the previous financial market boom now also bear the immense losses that have occurred—losses that should not fall on the vast majority of citizens who never benefited from the financial boom in the first place.
I believe that the socially just approach is currently also the only economically sensible option.
It is the only way to break through the devastating dynamics of the crisis.
Mr. President! In early March, the political prisoners Mikhail Khodorkovsky and Platon Lebedev were transferred from Chita in Siberia to Moscow to face renewed charges based on untenable accusations.

These important talks have now been postponed by the Russian side indefinitely.
Russia thereby clearly demonstrates the low value it places on human rights.
The judiciary continues to be used not only not for justice, but also to persecute regime opponents, thereby deceiving the EU.

has drawn my attention to the fact that this is now the third amendment to cosmetic legislation that I have had the opportunity to accompany—the sixth amendment, the seventh amendment, and now this amendment into a regulation.

Madam President, Madam Commissioner, esteemed colleagues! The report by my colleague Vanisterol on the Green Paper and the future shape of cohesion policy is undoubtedly one of the most significant produced by the Committee on Regional Policy in recent years.
We agree with the fundamental concept of the Green Paper, according to which territorial cohesion is indeed about addressing the polycentric development of
ensuring the overall sustainable development of the sovereign territories with their diverse characteristics and features, while simultaneously preserving their diversity.
It is correct that in the next planning period a more comprehensive system of phased assistance for transition regions crossing the threshold of 75
exceeding the gross domestic product, must be created, so that these regions obtain a clear status and thus have greater security in their development.
In the report by colleague Krehe on obstacles encountered in the activation of structural fund appropriations, the most significant shortcomings are rightly listed, which applicants face when

The use of structural funds is hindered, for example, by excessive administrative overhead, an excessive number of complex regulations, and slow, cumbersome centralized administration in the member states.
Various recommendations are made to the Commission regarding effective measures that should be taken to overcome these obstacles.
I would simply like to highlight and emphasize two such measures.
First, project managers are required to retain their project documentation for a period of ten years in order to have it available for inspections carried out by the Commission as well.
This is excessively bureaucratic for smaller projects.
Indeed, it is correct that this should now be reduced to a period of three years. And secondly, the evaluation criteria of the EU Commission for innovative projects are causing significant problems.
For innovative projects, different evaluation criteria must be applied than for other types of projects.
It is simply necessary that something different takes place here.

Madam President! If Europe takes money into its hands, and especially if the member states themselves put a great deal of money into their hands to assist the automotive industry in its current crisis, then the aim cannot merely be to preserve the status quo and prevent a collapse; rather, it involves a number of other concerns.
The most important ones have been mentioned.
The issue is that people seeking employment need not only to find jobs but also to secure them, and indeed do so on a long-term basis.
To achieve this, it is necessary that we promote new technologies, innovation, and, above all, sustainability within the transportation system to a greater extent than we have done so far.

We should therefore link all our measures to these objectives, so that we do not have to accept criticism in a few years' time. We should have thought about all this many years ago; then we would not be facing the next, new crisis.

Unfortunately, some amendment proposals attempt to supplement this suggestion with provisions that are incompatible with the objectives of the regulation or even seek to interfere with existing case law.

Madam President, Madam President, esteemed Commissioner, dear colleagues! Let me make one thing perfectly clear at the outset: the donation of food to those in need, particularly during these economically challenging times, is not up for debate in tomorrow’s vote on our colleague Tschicker’s report.
Therefore, I would like to make clear right at the beginning that it was not easy to find a common line within our parliamentary group.
Therefore, I respect each parliamentary colleague's personal decision to deviate from the rejection of the report as intended by us.
Why cannot I accept the outcome of the committee vote in this form?

There are a number of reasons for this, none of which have anything—absolutely nothing—to do with providing assistance to those in need within the European Union during these economically difficult times.

On the contrary, the amendment proposals submitted by my colleague Nils Busk on behalf of the ArtEktion group, though regrettably deemed inadmissible, were intended to place the existing system on a sustainable foundation.
For the existing system is an anachronism from times of agricultural overproduction, which thankfully belong to the past.
Thanks to the successful decoupling of direct payments, intervention stocks have steadily decreased in recent years, so that today up to 85 percent of foodstuffs must be purchased on the open market.

Policy coherence in the interest of development means that we do not take with one hand what we give with the other.

It makes little sense, for example, to channel development aid into building up agriculture in developing countries while simultaneously undermining local markets through direct or indirect export subsidies.

Likewise, no matter how strongly we advocate for better healthcare, we undermine our efforts if we hinder action through generic drugs or drive medication prices to unaffordable levels through patent extensions.

And biofuels may slightly reduce CO2 emissions in the EU, but they lead to deforestation and land-use changes in developing countries, resulting in increased climate change—precisely what we aimed to avoid—let alone the displacement of indigenous populations, loss of biodiversity, and reduction of arable land for food production.
Currently, we are, that is, the EU itself, not particularly cohesive.
That policy measures should not contradict one another has long been recognized by the EU in theory.
The Treaty of Lisbon enshrines policy coherence for development.
Article 208 states: "In implementing political measures that may affect developing countries, the Union shall take into account the objective of development cooperation."
We must measure ourselves against this requirement.
This year, we are reviewing progress toward achieving the Millennium Development Goals.
It is clear that without political coherence, we will not be able to fulfill our promises.

We must become aware of this responsibility, as all of us who adopt and implement legislation. Before we decide on a measure, there must be an impact assessment, so that we can recognize what negative effects European legislation might have on developing countries.
We need expertise in the Council, Commission, and Parliament that can identify inconsistencies.
The Commission's work programme on policy coherence represents a positive step forward.
And now it depends on how this plan is implemented.

Policy coherence in the interest of development means that in the context of fisheries reform we must take greater account of the interests of local communities and must not subordinate them to the interests of European fishing corporations.
It means that we must dry up the flow of funds from developing countries into tax havens.
It means that we cannot approve ACTA if there remains concern that this agreement could interfere with access to medicines or technologies in developing countries.
To date, the Commission has not been able to dispel this suspicion.

Policy coherence also means that, in establishing the External Service, development policy must remain a strong, independent policy field, and the competences of the Commissioner for Development must be expanded, not reduced.
And it also entails enabling Fairtrade, for example, in public procurement by municipalities, as my colleague Heide Rüle has proposed.
In many cases, we Members of Parliament must closely monitor the Commission and the Council to ensure they develop coherent policies.
In many cases, however, it also lies with us members of parliament to be coherent in the interest of development.
We need to improve our coordination between the committees, and we require either one or a permanent rapporteur.
The report, which the Development Committee unanimously adopted, makes numerous constructive suggestions and advances our progress significantly.
I would like to thank all shadow rapporteurs, the Ombudsman, and all the NGOs for their support and cooperation, and I hope that our joint report will likewise be adopted by the plenary tomorrow.

No, we do not want this, and that is precisely why we decided in July that we do not want cloned meat or any animal products derived from cloned animals.
And that is also only consistent.

Yes, we know that products from the cloned animals or their descendants are likely not harmful to health, and personally, I assume they would actually be suitable for human consumption. However, there is indeed no shortage of meat produced through conventional methods.
Why should we then get involved in this clone?
It is utterly unnecessary to carry out this animal cruelty; the population does not want it either.
The public does not want these products, and we do not need them either. There is no justification for cloned meat in the European Union.
Therefore, we want a clear regulation explicitly applicable to cloned animals and also to their offspring.
I want a ban on cloning and cloning products in the European Union, and I also believe that we can enforce this.
Of course, I must also apply this to imports.
I want to be certain that I am not secretly being served cloned meat, as it is not labeled, as has already happened here.
I don't believe one can trace back all meat products.
Beef mince is possible.
However, with pork, due to the large quantities produced, it will not be possible to trace whether there was actually a clone in the ancestral lineage.
Fundamentally, I think yes, it is a principle issue, it is an ethical question.
And colleagues have stated it too: We must also engage with ethical issues, and we are responsible for ethical questions as well.
Of course, there is the issue of animal welfare, but there is also another question.

Experience shows that as a livestock producer, I have studied this. Experience shows that all reproductive techniques, once they actually worked in animal production, were ultimately applied to humans.
We must therefore ask ourselves today: Will and can we accept cloning technology in the not-so-distant future? Will and can we also accept it in humans?
So I don't want that, and I can't do it either.
And even if in the future we might one day be able to clone an organ vital to life, perhaps even a clone of myself, I wouldn't want that.
I do not believe this constitutes an exaggerated horror scenario.
I believe that this would be possible in a future that is not at all significantly different, if we were to allow cloning.
Therefore, we urgently need a ban not only on cloning but also on cloning and its offspring throughout the entire EU.
I am not afraid of the WTO.
We can implement this.
We do not need to copy everything that the USA do, despite our transatlantic friendship.

My request to the Commissioner is to consider, once again, whether this four-year deadline, when presented to Parliament and if such a new argument arises in the Council, might not enable the Commission to show some flexibility.
I understood your comment just now to mean that consideration is being given within the Commission, and I thank you for that.

Mr. President, Madam Minister, Madam Commissioner! If we must draw a lesson from Copenhagen, where you, Madam Commissioner, are currently holding the presidency, it is that the European Union must speak with one voice at such conferences in the future.

There may be occasions on which we differ here and there. But if we Europeans already speak with different voices in that region, our influence will lack credibility; and if we lack credibility, then other people will not follow us.
Why should you?

That is why it is correct that the figures we agreed upon in the final year 2002 under Chancellor Merkel, and again in 2007 under Chancellor Merkel, must essentially have remained in place and represent the foundations of these negotiations.

I share your view that the ambitious targets of 2 degrees should not today be seriously questioned in favor of a significantly lower temperature threshold, as this would make the feasibility of these goals extraordinarily difficult and cause people to lose faith in them.
I am greatly concerned that we are calling our intellectual property into question.
Thus, our industry invests money and manpower to develop things.
If we now go ahead and loosen the fence around this intellectual property while leaving the existing gaps as they are, then we will in fact lose the valuable components of our own property.
I am certainly in favor of technology transfer, but our companies must also see a purpose in investing in it, and therefore intellectual property must not be diluted.
Regarding the obligation, I also tend to demand serious commitments from our partners worldwide.
But they must be credible, and they must be feasible for these countries.
Thus, we are doing exactly the opposite of what we want, namely that you will distance yourselves from us.
Being convincingly present in Cancún is an important point.
The major decisions will be made later in Johannesburg, but in Cancun, I believe we should prepare the ground.

Mr. President, Mr. President! There has been an incident during the debate on the European Council which I myself do not wish to address further, as I was the target of an insult.
But I want to say that there are levels on which I cannot be offended, and where I do not experience it as an offense, because to offend me, one must possess a certain degree of seriousness.
I would, however, like to sincerely thank you for the countless expressions of solidarity I have received since then here in this house.
I would like to sincerely thank you, dear colleagues from all factions.
I believe this is a sign of our shared commitment to community, democracy, and Europe, a conviction held by the overwhelming majority of this House.
My heartfelt thanks for that.

Dear Commissioner, dear Minister, dear Mr. Gss, dear colleagues and shadow rapporteurs! I am pleased that we have now taken this step, whereby for the first time a sector of the European financial system is placed under direct European supervision.
This is also a step toward a strong Europe within a single market, and toward strong oversight of a sector that contributed to the crisis.
I would like to make one remark on this, dear Mr. Vannier: the selection processes for the new agencies are still underway, and the new agencies will be no better than their personnel.

I can only hope that in the selection of personnel, genuine expertise, independence from the financial industry they are supposed to oversee, and a clear commitment to the public good will be the decisive factors, and that we will not see national quotas imposed again, overriding these crucial criteria.
Moreover, much still needs to be done in the area of rating agencies.

We still lack proper transparency regarding the information used by rating agencies. There is far too extensive use of regulatory ratings in the financial sector and in regulations governing the financial industry.
We face serious conflicts of interest; there is clearly market power held by three major providers, and there is no liability for ratings that are negligently produced.
Therefore, Mr Commissioner, I believe it is good that the consultation is currently underway, and we look forward to the results.
I also believe that civil society should participate in this consultation, but it must then be about making a courageous proposal.
I am already looking forward to your proposal.
Be as courageous as you have been so far, and let us jointly shape legislation next year that truly addresses, at a European level, the problems that continue to exist in the rating market.
That is what we owe to the citizens.
Mr. President, Mr. Minister, Mr. Barnier, colleagues! The damage that rating agencies can cause has been demonstrated by the past months since the beginning of this year.
There is therefore urgent need for action.
Therefore, I welcome the directive, and in particular I welcome the fact that oversight and registration of rating agencies will be entrusted to ESMA.
I believe this is an important and positive step forward.
However, numerous good and further-reaching proposals have been left behind during the negotiations on the report.
I would like to note this here.
Therefore, the mandate of ESMA remains the only genuine and positive innovation.
The proposals for a public data base to facilitate the creation of ratings have been dropped. Likewise, there is no mention of establishing a public rating agency.
The consistent and continuous review of established ratings by supervisory authorities has also fallen by the wayside.

The Commission had continued to propose supporting unsolicited ratings in order to overcome the disastrous consequences of mandatory rating requests for financial market products. Nothing has come of this either.
I hope that these aspects will be taken into account in the forthcoming reports on this subject, which Jean Paul Grosset and also you, Mr. Vannier, have mentioned.
Mr. President, Commissioner, dear colleagues, dear colleagues! First of all, my thanks to those who have worked on this report, particularly to my colleague Benson!
I would like to draw attention to two aspects and perhaps provoke a bit of reflection.
We agree that energy efficiency—using energy sparingly—is the simplest and most sensible solution.
The question is, how do we achieve this goal?
I would like to ask: What has actually been achieved by repeatedly approaching the public in parliament with ever-new and higher percentage figures?
Have we really achieved more and moved more?
Or why and at which points has energy been used more efficiently?
I learn that industries, which consume large amounts of energy, use energy sparingly because it saves them money and is economically advantageous for them.

I have learned that people will act differently when they know whether this device consumes more or less energy. So, if they can see the difference and perceive a personal benefit, they will choose accordingly.
It's not about using the method incorrectly by working with percentages, but rather about applying the method correctly by advancing through incentives.
Second question: The Commissioner has just very impressively and clearly explained how complicated it is merely to describe the term—what is this actually?
How does this vary among the member states?
The colleague has just pointed out that Member States also have very different situations—economically, geographically, entirely different.
Is it then the right answer to work with a number, with a binding number, or should something else come to mind?

I believe we need to move somewhat away from the outdated debate in energy policy that relies on simple figures, binding mandates, and supposedly miraculous solutions, as if these could achieve meaningful results. Clearly, we have not achieved enough.
Perhaps we should try the other approach of differentiation, one that takes into account the varying situational circumstances and responses, yet also achieves impact.
It is the effects that matter, not our state of well-being.
This regulation, which is being discussed today, has not received much attention in all committees for a long time.
But at the end, when the results were finally available, some people in this house woke up.
Some unfortunately did not appear for the discussion at this time. It would have been very, very interesting to hear your opinion as well.
As a former member of the convent, the spirit of the convent remains very, very vivid in my memory.

It has clearly demanded in many areas that the European Parliament must be granted certain rights in order to maintain and preserve its credibility both within the institutions and vis-à-vis citizens.
In this respect, what the convent had prescribed for us had still not yet been given.

I thank the Belgian Presidency.
He has genuinely put great effort into ensuring that something happens, and our rapporteur, who negotiated with heart and soul.

We now have a negative example in Hungary, where retroactive price caps were introduced for crisis taxation.
This is unacceptable, particularly the financial insurance and trading sectors, but energy is also significantly affected here.

I would also like to ask President Orbán to review these provisions, as they are entirely incompatible with the internal market concept for others.
It is important that the EU addresses the issues surrounding migration and asylum law.
The Commission has submitted appropriate proposals.
Currently, there is still no precise analysis regarding the implementation of existing legal regulations.
Hardly any mature calculations and analyses exist even for the new proposals.
I may state that the EPP unreservedly affirms the right to asylum and the right to protection for those in need of protection.
However, examining reality unfortunately also leads us to the problem that the right to asylum is being invoked for entirely different reasons or even deliberately abused.
The right to asylum and subsidiary protection is not an instrument for general immigration into the 27 member states.
We must also ensure that organized human traffickers are not given the opportunity to profit financially—into billions—from the fate of people through our asylum system.
Asylum procedures must be conducted in a qualified and careful manner.
Those who are persecuted must have the assurance of finding protection within the EU.
Therefore, the Commission has provided for corresponding obligations in the new accession treaties.
I consider many of these to be correct, such as interpretation services, appropriate medical treatment, and due consideration of special vulnerability.
I would, however, also like to mention points that I consider problematic.
The authorities of the Member States are given far too few opportunities to prevent abuse.
The possibility of conducting accelerated proceedings and border procedures should not be unduly restricted.

If an applicant seriously breaches their cooperation obligations, there are hardly any sanctioning options—quite the opposite. If he goes underground illegally, the Member State should no longer be able to terminate its procedure negatively. If he reappears, he should be granted an expanded range of procedural options.
Even in cases of clearly unjustified grounds, a streamlined procedure should be possible following the second subsequent application.
This means significant cost increases.
Legal advice in the form of a lawyer is also currently, according to the wording of the Commission's draft, a substantial increase in costs for the states.

I simply and straightforwardly ask the Commission to reconsider the consequences of its proposals for practical realities, financial impacts, and the challenges facing the administrations of the Member States.
We want a high standard, but it must also be feasible and must particularly avoid placing completely impossible tasks before especially burdened Member States.
Mr. President, thank you very much for allowing me to speak briefly once again.
I have a great request to make of you, Commissioner Füle.
I would like to begin by stating that false assumptions lead to incorrect cost estimates.
We currently do not have declining numbers in the area of asylum seekers, but rather significantly increasing numbers.
This is related to visa facilitation measures, for example, with Serbia and Macedonia.
We are currently also witnessing a significant increase in the abuse of asylum rights in an attempt to enter a country of the European Union.

If one starts from false assumptions—that costs in subsequent instances would be saved by upgrading the initial proceeding to a higher-level reform through contesting the first-instance decision—one automatically assumes that all Member States essentially have poor first-instance proceedings.
This is not the case, however.
If standards are generally raised, this will consequently result in increased costs.
An increase in quality standards for all involved parties, as well as the complicating of procedures, is likely to result in higher costs in practical reality. I am happy to discuss this further.

Mr. President, dear colleagues! This report credits the Commission with a successful competition policy.
It must not be forgotten, however, that unregulated competition can also contribute to rising unemployment and widening the gap between the rich and the poor.
Unimpeded competition still takes precedence over the social needs of people in the European Union.
The creed of free markets cannot obscure the fact that they not only repeatedly fail, but are incapable of establishing social justice in a lasting manner.
A few proceedings regarding distortions of competition and price-fixing do not solve this problem.
A fair and simultaneously social internal market can only function with strong and fair regulation.
More is urgently needed for this.
For example, it is not acceptable to continually subject public utility services to private economic competition.
The report provides recommendations for the Commission's future competition policy.
Much stronger consumer protection, the exclusion of public service provision from competition rules, and the control of cartels—all of this is urgently needed.
In addition, a contractual clause ensuring a secure social advancement would be necessary, one that stands on equal footing with competition rules in Europe.
Mr. President, ladies and gentlemen! It is indeed a moment of great significance that we, for the first time in the European Parliament, today commemorate the Holocaust of Oma and Sindi.
Let me begin with the farewell letter written in 1943 by a 14-year-old boy, Robert Reinhardt, prior to his deportation from a German children's home to Auschwitz.
He wrote: "I have found my parents again."
We are on the way to the concentration camp.
I have mentally come so far that I can bear death.
I would like to thank you once again for everything you have done for me.
Greetings to all, farewell in heaven!
Your Robert!

Ladies and gentlemen, this boy, like 500 other Roma and Sinti, never returned. And although Roma and Sinti, together with Jews, were among the first victims of industrialized mass murder, the full truth about this remains unspoken even today, 66 years later.
And complicity on the part of the majority society is often concealed.
We need the entire truth about this chapter of history.
The Holocaust against Roma and Sinti must be recognized and punished as a crime against humanity, so that this history will not be repeated.
We must combat the prejudices against these people with passion.
Racist attacks and acts of violence, such as those that occurred in Hungary the year before last, demand that we stand together, and we must do so with civilian courage.
Freedom and equality and solidarity are not the privilege of individuals, but the right of all, for human and civil rights are indivisible.
Dear esteemed ladies and gentlemen, colleagues! In my view, the issue now is to truly utilize the possibilities offered by the law
In order to fully exploit the potential of strong financial supervision in Europe, and we have doubts as to whether, institutionally, these possibilities are truly being fully utilized.
We identify procedural flaws: we have not received the candidate list in advance from the parliament, but only the final result.
It would be simple to send it now to the executive directors.

We clearly observe a lack of gender equality in the appointment of positions, and due to certain incidents in the process, we have doubts about whether the member states are genuinely staffing these institutions in the European interest.
And the additional points in our letter raise a fundamental question.
But beyond that, of course, it is naturally about the suitability of the individual persons, and each person must be assessed individually, based on their respective competencies.
There we see two major problems.

We have observed during the hearings that one of the candidates disputed that it is an European institution, instead referring to a network of supervisory authorities, and even when asked for clarification, was unwilling to correct this statement.
In response to the question of whether you are prepared to make all lobbying contacts with the financial industry transparent, we have received no clear statements.
We sent a letter to all three candidates, and for us, the voting outcome depends on whether we receive clear and comprehensive responses to it.
Mr. President, Mr. President! Our social democratic group wishes to submit the same motion.
However, I would like to recommend to my colleague Mauro and to this Honourable House that we express this more precisely.
We are dealing with an extremely large influx.

Italy, like any other country in the Mediterranean region, is certainly overwhelmed by the current problem and cannot cope with it alone. Italy will not be the only country facing the massive flow of refugees.
We can assume that Malta, Greece, and Spain will also be confronted with this problem in the foreseeable future.

Therefore, we kindly request that the title of the debate be formulated very precisely, namely a statement by the Commission on immediate measures by the European Union to support Italy and other affected EU countries in coping with the influx of refugees.
Madam President, Commissioner, dear colleagues! I warmly welcome the outcome of the conciliation procedure.

By implementing the regulation, it will be possible to ensure that, in the field of coach transportation, first, all passengers—including those with limited mobility—now enjoy a level of protection comparable to that of other transport modes.

achieved, secondly, a high overall level of consumer protection is ensured, and thirdly, due consideration is given to the specific characteristics that this is a transport sector predominantly shaped by small and medium-sized enterprises.

Thus, the extreme demands that would have rendered bus services nearly impossible due to excessive requirements have been dropped, just as have demands that would have completely excluded the majority of member states from the scope of the regulation.
Public transport services that benefit the general public must also provide a high standard of quality for disabled individuals and people with limited mobility.
They should have a right to assistance; for the sake of social integration, this should be free of charge.

Madam President! I would like to congratulate everyone, particularly my colleague Kanzan, for his efforts in crafting a compromise on this difficult file, a process which was both challenging and lengthy.
There were successes, which have already been listed, but there were also disappointments, such as the scope of application and other technicalities, which have already been outlined.
With this compromise for bus traffic, we have secured the right of passage for all transport data.
The next necessary step is to create a jointly coherent and efficient framework program for all passenger terminals.
Fair and clearly defined rights are of particular importance for the credibility of the EU among European citizens.
To be credible, the coherence of rights for the various modes of transport must therefore be strictly respected.
There is no alternative: fundamentally different criteria apply to the rights of air passengers than, for example, to those of train passengers.

Therefore, when developing a framework programme, it is particularly important that we do not focus on the lowest and weakest standards, but rather that we keep ambitious goals in sight, in the interest of the citizens.

Madam President, esteemed Mr. President, dear colleagues! We are in the midst of a major economic crisis. We've been talking about this for months—yes, that's true. But we usually discuss it only in headlines, and then continue here cheerfully as if nothing had happened.
I believe it is because we ourselves, in Parliament, do not act sufficiently concretely or honestly enough, but rather there is this kind of headline-driven politics that takes place.
What actually do we do to promote growth and stability?
Are we ourselves also contributing to handling finances more responsibly and wisely?
Do we truly conduct a critical review of whether we are making the right expenditures, or does each of us not come up with a new idea every day about where to spend more money?

If we lack the strength to focus financial resources on future-oriented projects—that means conflict, debate, and difficult decisions—then it makes no sense to talk about innovation and research while failing to provide the necessary means.
That is relatively dishonest.

And if we want to stimulate the economy, we must also pursue a policy that stimulates the economy, rather than spending every hour from morning till night contemplating how to disrupt industry or the economy.
We cheerfully continue adopting resolutions, constantly demanding new resolutions today that disrupt the development of the economy.
Do you really believe that the question of binding energy efficiency targets is merely an example of a contribution to driving economic growth?
Do you really believe that we have stimulated the economy with our decisions, often taken in the context of energy?
Or have we disturbed them.
I wish we could speak a little more honestly about this, and then make fewer decisions, but ones that truly move things forward.
This is more demanding and laborious, and conflict will also become more likely, but that is the essence of democracy.
Mr. President, ladies and gentlemen! The demand for a financial transactions tax is not new. For several years now, opponents of globalization have been calling for such a tax.
Yet just five years ago, hardly anyone would have believed that it could be implemented in the foreseeable future.
But this week it is on the agenda of the European Parliament alongside a CO2 tax and eurobonds, and I think that is a good thing.
The financial instruments proposed by Annie Polimata in her report can only be welcomed and supported by me, and I believe we will also vote on them accordingly.
They are urgently necessary instruments.
The consolidation of public budgets so far has focused solely on cutting expenditures.
However, cuts in public spending primarily affect workers, pensioners, and the poor—those who depend on the welfare state.
The revenue side of the public sector has so far been completely overlooked.
She is the second lever that can be adjusted to consolidate public budgets.
Above all, the main contributors to the public debt crisis and the repayment of public debt can be appropriately engaged on the revenue side.
To consolidate public budgets, an increase in tax revenues is simply unavoidable.

In fact, the high level of public debt is primarily a consequence of the nationalization of private debts, namely the assumption of private banks' debts and the public sector's financing of the ensuing financial crisis.
The states are by no means solely responsible for the debt crisis.
It is therefore not only natural but also politically necessary to hold the financial sector accountable.
With the financial transaction tax, this sector would finally be made accountable as the primary cause of public debt and involved in repaying these debts.
This is an important political signal conveyed by this report, provided it is adopted in the form in which it has been presented.
But we likewise consider Eurobonds to be a sensible and therefore necessary instrument.
They contribute more to reducing debt than all sanctions or well-intentioned advice, however good.

The complaints of some surplus countries that their interest burdens are thereby increased by Eurobonds are, however, unacceptable, as these surplus countries simultaneously benefit from their exports to deficit countries.
This can then be criticized as a transfer union.
Anyone who advocates a social Europe and wants the EU to remain united in the future must at least broadly support a transfer union.
Finally, I would like to say to the Commission that I hope you will now finally submit the proposals for a financial transaction tax, which have been demanded for over a year.
Mr. President, ladies and gentlemen! The rescue shields were intended to protect EU member states against insolvency.
Yet they impose brutal austerity programs on the people and let the culprits go unpunished. Furthermore, the Commission is now also intervening in national wage policies.
Dear Hahn, it's absurd, and it brings tears to one's eyes to hear this formal argumentation claiming that none of this was ever meant to be socially political in the first place.
This position is entirely unacceptable. Wage policy is not the responsibility of the EU. This policy of the Commission is in blatant contradiction to the very idea of this European Union.
The European Union has never been a project for downward wage competition and social dumping.
There is talk of it, there is reading about wage rigidity, which should be reduced.
This means to speak: Wages should reflect market conditions.
Ladies and gentlemen, blind men talking about color—those who formulate such statements have absolutely no understanding of collective bargaining systems.
They interfere with collective bargaining autonomy and reduce the much-prized autonomy of the social partners and social dialogue to absurdity.

I wish to make it clear that this Parliament is called upon to ensure the preservation of autonomous collective bargaining policy, to protect social dialogue, and to prevent any further erosion of social protection.
We need measures against a counterproductive austerity policy. Thank you.

has Mrs. Gabriele Zimmer the floor? I would actually like to ask the two representatives from the Council and the Commission why you believe you need to respond to this issue using speech bubbles.

I believe it would help nobody if I were to speak quite explicitly about the fact that, precisely in preparation for the Istanbul Conference, the Member States of the European Union and the European Union
They must finally fully meet their obligations regarding development aid, and it cannot in any way be tolerated that member states are increasingly evading this responsibility.
It is unacceptable that one billion people still go hungry every day, the majority of whom live in LDCs, the poorest countries in the world.

It is unacceptable that a large part of our development aid does not go toward promoting and building agricultural structures, especially given that a billion people are hungry, and that this share has rapidly declined in recent years.
And then talking about hoping that the LDC will become more shock-resistant. I'm starting to find that questionable.
I urge you to address the problems in a concrete and clear manner and to undertake specific commitments to genuinely assist the LDCs.
In the context of the internal market, public procurement law is a key component, as the awarding of public contracts is essential.

Mr. Barnier, if you say that in this context you also wish to promote social integration, then I must ask you: I have heard little about protection of social rights, little about protection of collective agreements.
This belongs to the fundamental rules that, as you yourself said, you do not wish to question.

If one wishes to uphold fundamental principles, future public procurement must ensure that basic social standards are protected, collective wage agreements are not undermined, and wage dumping is prevented.
This is part of a modern, modernized public procurement law.
I expressly state that social fundamental rights must no longer be subordinated to economic freedoms and free competition.
Either we want a social Europe, or we want permanent conflict with the people who turn away from this vision of Europe, feeling that their livelihoods are not being protected within it. Thank you.

Madam President, esteemed Commissioner K Cruz! Radio frequencies represent a valuable and finite resource that must be managed with care, foresight, and consideration of societal interests as a whole.
Therefore, the Radio Spectrum Policy Programme holds particular significance.
Radio frequencies must be used efficiently, taking into account economic, social, and cultural aspects.
To achieve this, technical possibilities must be fully utilized across all band areas in order to generate the highest possible efficiency.

Based on radio frequency policy, the telecom package adopted last year must be maintained, as it also established that radio frequency management by the EU and its member states must equally serve cultural, social, and economic interests.
This must be particularly taken into account when harmonizing frequency usage within the EU.
In this context, I would like to point out that the implementation of the digital dividend so far has been carried out at very different speeds across the Member States.
A further opening of the UHF band, particularly below 800 megahertz, should therefore only be considered after taking into account the experience gained from clearing this band.
Many technical and financial issues have not yet been conclusively resolved.

The issue of interference and the question of cost compensation for cultural institutions and local authorities during technical retrofitting, for example in the field of wireless microphones, remains unresolved to a satisfactory extent.
When allocating frequency bands, different interests must be responsibly balanced against one another.

Comprehensive coverage with mobile broadband networks is a fundamental requirement in the interest of European citizens. At the same time, continued development in the field of public and private broadcasting must also be taken into account, as an important instrument for information and cultural diversity.
We must set the course correctly for technological development, Europe's positioning in the field of digital information technology, and cultural diversity.
Mr. President, dear Mr. Rehn! I would first like to say that you have indeed described it: the situation has worsened.
The eurozone, the gap is widening between countries that are recovering and those that are performing more weakly.
And the costs of supporting the weaker countries continue to rise steadily.

In this situation, I have a few questions for you. As the Monetary Commissioner, I ask you: Over the past two days, customers of the ECB system have accumulated liabilities and claims between central banks amounting to more than 400 billion euros.
This is because banks continue to be insufficiently financed by the capital market.
How do you assess these liabilities that are not provided for within the eurosystem?
The major problem why we are unable to make further progress with restructuring the debt is that the banking system, as Frau Ford also stated, is not properly structured.
The proposal for a European restructuring system has been delayed.
When will the Commission present it?
And as Economic and Monetary Commissioner, how do you assess this delay?
What do you do to make it go faster?

Then we saw, as Green Party members had already predicted long before, that these measures against Greece would not work in Portugal either, as long as the macroeconomic conditions in the eurozone remain unchanged.
We have rising interest burdens, which are still absolutely rising, from a macroeconomic perspective for these countries.
We are witnessing increasing capital exports and capital flight into some of these countries.
We continue to experience insufficient demand from various European partner countries, particularly from the stronger eurozone countries.
And we have rising resource and food prices, at least when one excludes the most recent fluctuations.

Besides the bailout for these countries, besides the financial support measures, I wonder what steps are being taken to enable the weaker countries to manage their macroeconomic policies effectively—on the demand side from abroad, and in terms of relief regarding interest rates?
And how
I would therefore encourage you to push for a strong package that improves the macroeconomic conditions of these countries within the eurozone.

Lastly, in my view, the measures have gone against the spirit of the EU Treaty in countries such as Greece, Portugal, and Spain, particularly affecting the most vulnerable, while the stronger economies have so far not required the wealthier segments of their populations to contribute to the burden.
What measures will you take to ensure that the wealthy are also engaged in contributing to the costs of this crisis?
Mr. President, Mr. President! Ms. Gia Gönz is a member of my parliamentary group.
One can be against Feren Jorzani, one can be for Feren Jorzani.
One can either reject Kingergg as a politician or support her.
This is certainly something that any individual can freely and independently determine: whether one holds a political conviction against it.
The debate we are conducting here is about the last dictatorship remaining in Europe.
We lead you through one of the harshest and bloodiest dictators Europe has ever known.
I find it absolutely unacceptable that a democratically elected member of my parliamentary group is being compared to a bloodthirsty dictator.

I believe that Parliament must also reject this, particularly from a representative of a party that itself is characterized by inflicting hatred and persecution upon the Roma minority in Hungary, a level of which is unmatched across Europe.

Mr. President, dear colleagues, dear visitors! The cultural diversity of Europe is a valuable asset that must be preserved and further developed in the years to come.

To make this possible, we must confront the challenges of the digital age not only within the information and knowledge society, but above all within the sphere of culture and the creative industries.
Cultural diversity is not only an idealistic value but also a significant economic factor.
Around five million people are employed in the cultural sector in the EU.
This corresponds to approximately 2.5 percent of total employment.
The cultural sector is a growth sector that is developing more rapidly than many areas of the rest of the economy.

The development of information and communication technologies is driven just as much by the content offered by the cultural sector as the form and quality of content have been transformed and further developed by technology.
We therefore need framework conditions for the culture and creative industries that enable them to develop sustainably, given the challenges of the digital age.
We need a modern, accessible, and legally secure system in which everyone can equally develop and thrive.
Culture and the creative industries are a driving force for Europe.
Therefore, we must also pay attention to this area in the field of school and vocational education.
We must impart cultural and creative competencies to young people, as well as in the offerings of lifelong learning.
We must also strengthen the entrepreneurial skills of cultural creators, not only entrepreneurial competencies but also solid media literacy in the field of new technologies.
We need framework conditions in which culture creators can secure their livelihood.
Therefore, we will need to engage intensively with issues relating to copyright, social security, and intellectual property, as well as open access to information and content, in order to the

to ensure the creative and cultural industries in the future within a digital environment and to preserve the cultural diversity and cultural heritage of Europe as a fundamental characteristic of the European Union.
Mr. President, dear colleague Kelllam, dear colleagues from the Baltic States, dear colleagues! Today, we particularly remember the expulsions and mass deportations from the Baltic States.
These expulsions belong to the darkest events in European history.
Indeed, the past century was not only, and not least precisely for this reason, rightly referred to as a century of expulsions.
We bow our heads today in respect to the victims of these inhumane acts.
In this context, we demand a thorough clarification of Soviet history, and in particular of the origins of Stalinism.

Today's Russia should not perceive this as a political attack, but rather as support for coming to terms with its own history, which, like the history of other European countries, is marked by both bright and dark chapters.
But just as we think of the victims from the Baltic States, we also think of the Armenians who were expelled from Turkey at the beginning of the following century.
We consider the so-called population exchange between Turkey and Greece.
A particular high point of deportations in Europe was reached in the middle of the century by the Nazi regime and the communist regime of the Soviet Union.
The expulsions after the Second World War also inflicted deep wounds, as did the expulsions during the Yugoslav wars.
We must also not forget that many colonial regimes were characterized by expulsion.
We must demand a serious engagement with the various forms of displacement and the spurious justifications behind them.
Punishment also includes clarifying guilt and punishing the guilty, to the extent that this is still possible, as is currently the case, for example, in the Mlade affair.
But the decisive answer lies in European unity, precisely through and with the independent Baltic states, and a clear commitment to continuing the still-unfinished process of European integration.
This historical responsibility is what we owe not only to ourselves, but especially to the victims of the numerous expulsions across Europe.
Many female politicians from all countries across Europe, from East and West, have worked on it.
For example, consider the solemn declaration signed by Polish President Weisniewski and German Federal President Rau.
And since we wish to learn from the past, we must remain vigilant, even toward early signs of expulsions, such as in the case of the Roma.

We must reject all statements such as the claim that one should send Arab migrants back across the Mediterranean in boats, and we must resist those who seek to undo Europe's achievements of borderless cooperation and wish to revive a new narrow-minded nationalism.
Let us bow in Erfurt, above all in response to the expulsion and deportation, but let us unanimously affirm our commitment to continuing European integration as the only genuine guarantee.
