Madam President, Commissioner, esteemed ladies and gentlemen, dear colleagues! I had the opportunity to serve as a parliamentary election observer during the referendum on South Sudan's independence, and I was deeply impressed by the strong desire of the South Sudanese people to determine the future of their country themselves.
I also observed the great sense of expectation among the people who regarded the independence of their country as liberation from slavery.

Thousands, perhaps tens of thousands, of so-called internally displaced persons, who had been driven by decades of conflict in the south to refugee camps in the north, set out for their former homeland in southern Swedana.

Of course, the people of South Sudan, beyond their desire for the country's independence, primarily wish to pursue a path toward a happier future through peaceful coexistence, after decades marked by violent conflicts in which millions of South Sudanese lost their lives.
On July 9th, the new state of South Sudan is set to become officially independent, thereby expanding the family of nations in our world.
The state of affairs is unimaginably poor, and this has already been discussed extensively here.
Eighty percent of the people live as semi-nomadic herders; 80 percent are illiterate. The conflict over Abillé escalates into a military conflict.
There were already dead and wounded people there.

To make matters worse, rivalries between tribes and armed groups in South Sudan are also escalating, and according to observers, more than a thousand people have already lost their lives since January.
The wounded and those who have been violated are scarcely counted.
The Sudan was already among the failed states.
There is a risk that the state of South Sudan is already beginning to function as a failed state.
I am firmly convinced that Europe has a major responsibility toward these regions.
Given the colonial past of those countries, joint action with the UN and the African Union is required here, and Europe can also demonstrate its common foreign policy in this context.
It is not only about money, but also about supporting the delicate, emerging civil society.

We must negotiate firmly with those in whose hands the fate of the country lies—namely, the tribal leaders and commanders of military groups—so that they do not disappoint the hopes of their people in the country. Thank you.

Madam President, Commissioner, ladies and gentlemen! I would like to express my sincere gratitude to my colleague Bartolotti for initiating this inquiry, thereby bringing Mongolia onto the agenda for debate in the European Parliament.
While the former Soviet republics of Central Asia are now addressed in parliament from various perspectives, milk production and its situation here have received only scant attention.

It was quite unjust, however, that Mongolia was the one that, as the starting point of the former communist bloc, early on seized its independence and embarked on a path toward democratizing its society.

In 1992, the Mongols adopted a democratic constitution, and since then several elections have taken place, which, according to observers, have generally proceeded in a democratic and orderly manner—a circumstance that is far from self-evident for countries in this region.

For several years, the Mongolian government has been striving to combat corruption in law enforcement, and in 2010 it issued a moratorium on the death penalty—something that even some major democratic states have not achieved to this day.
Nevertheless, the three million inhabitants of this geographically vast country, dominated by extreme climatic conditions, continue to suffer the consequences of an ongoing transformation process.
Thus, a rural population that is still predominantly nomadic is moving into the few cities that are not infrastructure-ready for such influx.
The country's mineral resources are extracted in an environmentally sound manner.
Energy problems hinder civilian and economic life.
Trapped between the two large neighbors, Russia and China, Mongolia risks becoming even more dependent on these states than before.

The efforts and achievements of Mongolia—here I see the glass half full, not half empty. In regard to the democratization of society, it should be acknowledged that they serve as exemplary for this region and demonstrate what is possible despite a difficult past.
Mongolia should continue to receive our support, not only within the framework of aid programs, but also by the European Union establishing a direct presence in the Mongolian capital through its own

Debates Following the most difficult technical negotiations and political trilogues, the political package is now ready for voting. It represents a major compromise between the European Parliament and the Council of Ministers on the future labelling of foodstuffs.
This regulation aims to provide consumers with more and better information about food products, enabling them to make informed purchasing decisions.
But that is not all. The European food industry should also benefit.
Legal certainty, final bureaucratic resolution, and improved lawmaking are the objectives.
This is particularly important for small and medium-sized enterprises, and it should be noted that the food sector in the European Union consists of more than 80 percent SMEs.
At the same time, this labeling must be applicable to every food product and every non-alcoholic beverage, ranging from basic staple foods to highly processed ready-to-eat meals.
That is why a mandatory labeling in traffic-light colors is not feasible, as this type of labeling simply does not fit basic foodstuffs.

Despite all difficulties, despite all political and ideological differences in Parliament, and despite all national egoisms of the member states, the compromise now achieved can nevertheless stand up to scrutiny.
Allergens will be recognizable at first glance.
The plant origin of plant oils is indicated.
There is a labeling requirement for the origin of pork, poultry, lamb, and goat meat.

For further products, an impact assessment will determine whether and how origin information can be provided, for example for milk and dairy products, single ingredients—meaning products consisting of a single main ingredient—meat in processed foods
Products, etc., that are imitations must be clearly marked on the front side, and the same applies to formed meat and formed fish made by assembling or gluing together small pieces.
A package may not give the impression that it contains something that is not actually inside.
If a picture of fruit appears on a yogurt container, the yogurt must actually contain fruit, not just food coloring and flavoring agents.
In addition, the mandatory declaration of the main nutrients must always be provided per 100 grams or 100 milliliters for reasons of comparability.
Added water in meat must be indicated, the date of first freezing for meat and fish, and many other details.
Above all, all information must be legible.

We have defined readability, we have established a minimum font size, and the Commission has been tasked with setting binding rules for further factors, such as the contrast between the text and the background.
At the same time, we protect craftsmanship at high costs and bureaucratic expense.
Unpackaged fresh products are required to provide allergen information, but not the complete nutritional information, which would require expensive analyses.
The Commission originally wanted this, but that would have destroyed our national and regional particularities and specialities, leading to a European uniform porridge, and therefore we prevented it.
But now we have all agreed, and the reputable consumer protection organizations are quite satisfied with what has been achieved.

I therefore thank all those involved, the Commission's shadow rapporteurs, particularly Commissioner Dalli for his explanations, which I hope he will shortly deliver and which will untie the knot that has formed in our political discourse.

Madam President, Commissioner, dear colleagues! Given the current exceptional situation resulting from the financial, economic, and budgetary crisis, decisions at the European level should focus on urgent and essential issues.
Only in this way can we restore the trust of our citizens in the seriousness of our actions.
In my report, I discuss the strategy for preventing infinite human suffering resulting from approximately 35 deaths per year and more than
5 million serious injuries resulting from road traffic accidents, as well as the prevention of an associated annual economic loss of 130 billion euros.
The number of deaths alone equals that which would result from the crash of 250 fully loaded medium-sized passenger aircraft.
What would we not do to prevent this?
Accident-related follow-up costs significantly exceed the annual total budget of the European Union.
Therefore, I am confident that it is appropriate for us to engage sustainably in this field.

Thanks to the constructive work of all shadow rapporteurs as well as many colleagues, we have for the first time succeeded in developing a comprehensive, coherent approach to improving road traffic safety through this report.

It takes into account the contents of the UN Road Safety Action Plan 2010–2020 as well as the objectives and activities of road safety efforts by EU member states and broader sectors of civil society.
It includes, first, ambitious but realistic goals for the period up to 2020 and, beyond that, the vision SEERROM.
Accordingly, the number of fatalities from accidents is to be reduced by 50% by 2020, the number of child fatalities by 60%, and the number of seriously injured by 40%.
This is in the fundamental interest of each individual person among us, as well as in the interest of our economy.
We recommend that Member States break down these objectives and methods to the level of their territory and expect them to develop their own road safety action programmes.
Secondly, it specifies who is responsible for the individual activities and measures.
This is by far not only the European Union.

In line with the principle of subsidiarity, the holistic approach means that national states, regional or local authorities, and indeed every individual road user, must assume responsibility in accordance with their respective duty to cooperate.
Thirdly, it encompasses how, that is, with which instruments, activities, and measures, the objectives are to be achieved.
Legislative proposals at the European level are only one strategy.
We cannot and do not wish to dispense with guidelines, recommendations, or instructions.
Particular attention has been paid to the principle of subsidiarity. This means that responsibilities and competences have been clearly defined, even in cases where they are shared.
We appreciate the Commission's diverse activities.

However, since you come from a wide variety of Directorates-General such as Transport, Economic Affairs, Consumer Protection, Environment, Communication, Research and Development, the Internal Market, and others, your positions are not always coherent, often uncoordinated, and sometimes even contradictory.
Therefore, we propose the establishment of a coordinator for road traffic safety within the European Commission.
This avoids the establishment of an agency for road traffic safety in a sustainable manner, but greatly increases the efficiency of the European Commission.
Both the selection of measures and their evaluation require a scientifically sound approach based on comparable, high-quality data definitions and statistics.

We therefore call, among other things, for a harmonized analysis of injury and accident causes, as well as the exchange of data across the EU, while maintaining a high level of personal data protection.
Six main action fields have been identified.
They concern the human as a road user, traffic regulations, vehicles, infrastructures, their intelligent networking, as well as the special consideration of vulnerable road users.
I request approval for tomorrow.

Corresponding forms are available at Swiss banks.

This agreement clearly lacks not only the necessary alignment at the EU level, but also runs counter to the EU's efforts to increase transparency in tax matters. The aim is to make tax collection in the member states more efficient and, above all, to combat and prevent tax fraud.
Therefore, in our view, this cannot be acceptable.

Thus, this definition encompasses not only document but also information.
Information from ongoing interinstitutional proceedings, such as informal trilogue negotiations, should also be made public.

Madam President, Commissioner, esteemed colleagues! It surprises me that we continually have to remind ourselves of what is actually meant by structural and cohesion policy.

Cohesion and structural policy is one of the European Union's central policy areas, and by definition aims to strengthen economic and social cohesion within the Union, particularly promoting growth and employment in underdeveloped regions.
The necessity and success of cohesion and structural policy have been particularly evident in the years of the crisis.
In many regions, economic development would appear much more difficult were these funds not available.

The implementation of the funding structure is carried out on the basis of operational programs, which have already been mentioned, in which the funding strategy for the respective region is described.
The European Union, its member states, and regions collaborate here in a partnership, thereby creating genuine European added value.

Therefore, I am greatly surprised whenever, time and again, Member States—and also, as we have just seen, the Commission itself—attempt to treat structural funds as a savings book to be drawn upon for all kinds of current policy initiatives.

This does not do justice to the spirit and purpose of cohesion policy and, moreover, undermines the actors in the regions who are already working towards achieving the goals based on long-term strategies and agreements.
Certainly, combating youth unemployment and promoting economic growth are important goals.
This will be achieved only if the cooperative partnership among the regions, the Member States, and the Commission is maintained and if the actors in the regions are involved and remain involved.
Because only you possess the knowledge of what needs to be done in your regions.
Cohesion and structural policy constitute the truly partnership-based, solidarity-driven link in the actions undertaken across the various policy levels within the European Union.
It makes visible the solidarity of Europe at the local level within the regions, and should not be abandoned carelessly due to political quick fixes.

If we give young people the feeling of absolute uselessness, we should not be surprised that they turn away from this EU, as they lack reasonable prospects for work and life.

Mr. Commissioner, I urgently request that you ensure decisive countermeasures are initiated. I already perceive quite a bit, but please ensure that the long-standing intellectual stillness does not now give way to operational frenzy!

When I read that the Commission is launching a program under which young people applying abroad should now also receive financial support, then we are merely organizing a traveling circus that does not solve the problem.
On the contrary, it becomes counterproductive; then technicians, doctors, engineers, and nursing staff are lured away from countries that are wealthier and not affected by this crisis.

What is now required in this crisis is to take proactive measures to address the root cause of this development, namely the elimination of this structural distortion, which is hampering the economy in Europe.
We need a targeted growth policy; we need to strengthen domestic purchasing power, and public investments in education, research, and alternative energies.
That would be the way.
With the combination of European funding, it would then be possible to actually develop an integrated concept that provides young people with a perspective on life.
Final remark: This youth unemployment is also incompatible with the absurd idea of wanting to raise the retirement age to 70 for people.
We need a different policy that provides young people with access to the labor market.
Therefore, I urgently request thanks.

The reform of the Generalized System of Preferences was indeed necessary.

The Commission unfortunately refers only to the World Bank classification.
The Parliament has repeatedly and critically addressed gross domestic product as the sole criterion in the past.
The definition of countries with upper-middle income may refer to countries with a GDP per capita of 4,000 or up to 12,000 U.S. dollars.

However, this category of countries also includes many poorer nations, such as Namibia and Ecuador.
To date, the Commission had at least still invoked the vulnerability criterion, meaning countries that export little or only very few goods.
This at least avoids the situation in which countries that depend less on the export of goods and are highly vulnerable are classified too highly.
Why then did the Commission remove this criterion?
Countries such as the Dominican Republic, Fiji Islands, Gabon, and Botswana will thus be excluded, even though their economies fall under the criterion of vulnerability.
Why did the Commission not continue using the old criteria, which were already somewhat inadequate?
There is a growing suspicion that the Commission is trying to force poorer countries into free trade agreements that are unfavorable to them.

However, the preference system is an instrument for promoting development and cannot be replaced by purely trade-oriented instruments, in which case the development status of a country or region would no longer play any role at all.

The new regulation, however, also results in trade regions being torn apart.

Mr. President, esteemed Commissioner, dear colleagues! In June 2010, the heads of government of the EU finally recognized that the European Community requires a more closely coordinated financial and economic policy.

A single market without coordination in economic wage and social policy places member states under considerable temptation to generate short-term growth and employment at the expense of their neighbors.
Such imbalances can economically as well as politically jeopardize the currency union.
And precisely such a situation is the EU currently facing.

For years, the German economy has drained its neighbors by relying on no impulses from investment or wage growth. Instead, Berlin has nearly driven the eurozone into ruin with a decade of aggressive export policies.
Therefore, it is encouraging that the Commission is now gradually addressing this core issue of the euro crisis in its country-specific recommendations and making corresponding suggestions.

It then transmits a predefined minimal data set of information to a rescue coordination center.
Uniform standards and specifications across Europe will ensure that these data are recognized, assessed, and handled as emergency calls upon arrival.

We want this eCall emergency call system to be based on the EU's unified emergency number 112, to be free of charge for users, to function throughout the EU, and to be compatible with emergency call systems of neighboring third countries.

whether the Union will actually have a future, or whether it will prove itself—and this would be, in my view, a historic defeat—as a footnote in history.

I have long missed this in official speeches: that it is about people, about those who are not responsible for our current crisis, yet bear the greatest burdens.
It will be difficult to implement this approach precisely.

You exercise it by advocating for your rights and by ensuring that the Council and the European institutions are fully aware of this responsibility.

One final point, which I would also like to mention, in order to emphasize once again that everything you have named as your priorities is important to us, and that we will support those points which move in this direction, as I have just defined them.

Dear colleagues! I believe we bear a very important responsibility here, especially as joint rapporteurs.
Madam Colleague, it will be extremely important for you to work swiftly and reliably on this file in the coming weeks and months.

At present, eyes are focused on us and on the Council to take this step, overcoming the banking crisis in Europe through another step toward European integration—namely, through a European Banking Union.
To this it must be said that the Parliament already demanded this two years ago with a broad majority.
We have conducted 26 trilogues.
We have taken a step in this direction, but unfortunately not as far as was actually necessary.
Additional billions of euros have arisen as a result.
It is positive that the Council has now drawn the necessary conclusions and wishes to take this step further to deepen the eurozone.
It is praiseworthy that the Commission has developed the proposals so quickly.
However, the Council's decision that the ECB should be the location of eurozone supervision is associated with certain difficulties.
In the Council, this leads to unanimity and results in the Parliament not having co-decision power on this file.
Based on the experience with the supervisory package, this is not acceptable.

Therefore, it is important that we proceed swiftly here, and that we negotiate this proposal on changes to the ECB as a package with the necessary adjustments to the existing supervisory framework, thereby effectively establishing the principle of democratic involvement of Parliament.
Democratically, there are further challenges in addition.
The ECB is independent in monetary policy, but not in fiscal policy, for which it currently has no responsibilities.

If you are now responsible for banking supervision, the Parliament will need full budgetary rights, full oversight rights, rights of questioning, and appointment rights, and I am confident that the Parliament will secure these rights with a broad majority.

Beyond these complex issues concerning the internal market, consumer protection, and the balance between European authority and financial responsibility, we will thoroughly address them in the coming weeks. Thank you.
By proposing to exclude developing countries from preferential market access, the Commission seeks to push these countries into free trade agreements.

These partnerships, as the term "economic partnerships" suggests, are not truly about mutual cooperation but focus solely on the interests of the EU, not on the development priorities of the partner countries.
This runs completely counter to the EU's goal of reducing poverty and aligning all external policy instruments with this objective.
Countries such as Cameroon, Côte d'Ivoire, and Namibia would be severely affected by this restriction.
Regional integration projects such as those in Southeast Africa would collapse if Kenya were to enter into such an agreement.
The Southern Cooperation would come to an end.
We cannot advocate for free trade in the world while merely meaning that other countries should kindly purchase our goods.
If the Commission truly believes that trade can promote development, then it should not obstruct it.
The Development Committee of this House has recommended rejecting the Commission's proposal.
We should support this in plenary session.

Madam President! The new directive establishes a framework for handling books, newspapers, artworks, films, musical compositions, and TV productions that are protected by copyright, but for which the rights holder cannot be identified or located.
Accordingly, books, films, photographs, or other intellectual creations for which no rights holder can be identified are to be made available to the public.
And thus, a cultural treasure is unearthed.
For us in the Committee on Culture, it was particularly important in this legislative initiative to emphasize the special significance of the diversity of the cultural heritage of the European Union.

In the archives of our museums, libraries, publishers, and broadcasting institutions, countless works remain unpublished, and merely because the rights holders are unknown, they have so far not been made available.
This changes with the directive.
It will enable future use of this cultural asset, provided that a thorough search is conducted beforehand.
This is a very important point, so that it is not said lightly that we find rights holders not.
For a balance had to be struck with the actual rights of creators of works and the public's need to be able to use such works where the correct situation cannot be clarified.
A particular emphasis for us also lies in ensuring that both known and unknown rights holders are fully protected going forward.
And where rights holders cannot be identified, the draft makes the use of digitized orphan works possible.
Projects such as Europeans and other major European digitization initiatives, as well as our museums, archives, educational institutions, and public broadcasting, will be involved.
In the future, it must also be about opening up orphan works in the archives of private publishers to the public.
In my view, this draft guideline and the compromise now reached truly represent a compromise and a first step in the right direction.
I believe that the cultural heritage associated with him holds immense potential.
We should continue working on this when revising the directive in two years.

Nordseewerk in Emmen was forced to file for insolvency last Wednesday, as a guarantee provided by the Lower Saxony state government was unexpectedly not extended.

Mr. President, Mr. Commissioner, dear colleagues! I believe that with today's debate on MiFID we are addressing the core area of financial market regulation.
this legislative term, the issue is about regulating financial markets, not merely focusing on individual products as we have done in the past.
I would like to mention only a few key points.
We didn't make things very easy for each other in our work last year.

We conducted a hearing in the committee, will carry out interviews with all stakeholders from all interested groups, and based on this, will develop a result through various processes, working documents, and similar materials, which we will present to the plenary for voting today and tomorrow.
I would like to highlight a few points.
Topic number one: Market structure.

On the one hand, we must naturally acknowledge that she has done quite a lot in recent years in the field of financial markets; however, our interest as Parliament is that every trade in financial instruments takes place within the MiFID rules. Therefore, we also support, under many conditions, a new category

We have approved the so-called organised trading facilities, where only non-equities are to be traded, where proprietary trading is to be prohibited, and where, according to the explanations regarding execution and transaction and customer interaction, appropriate execution at own discretion is possible.
I believe that by doing so we will make an important contribution toward ensuring that the entire segment of OTC instruments traded is conducted within the MiFID framework.
Another major issue, the so-called algorithmic trading or high-frequency trading, which was not addressed in the previous MiFID.

Here too, our aim was to establish clear rules of the game, ensuring that this area— which can be extremely dangerous and is also susceptible to manipulation—is subject to strict regulations including minimum holding periods.

Possibilities for trade interruption, enabling the testing of algorithms, allowing oversight structures to examine algorithms, introduction of so-called TIC size regime, minimum price thresholds, and corresponding fee systems.
Another topic I would like to address is the area of warning term markets.

I do believe we bear a special responsibility here in ensuring that the speculative component in genuine futures markets is restricted to such an extent that we do not contribute, through incorrect regulations, to the situation where people in the third and fourth worlds can no longer afford to buy food.
That is why we have adopted a two-part approach, aiming to curb speculation while avoiding harm to the real economy.
With position limits, with strict upper bounds on the number of contacts or positions, we aim to achieve this.
We also want to ensure, through a so-called position check system, that those who can demonstrate they genuinely need a product do not suddenly engage speculatively in this market.
Another issue that occupies us greatly is the area of investor protection.
I would like to point out here only that we have adopted a very broad approach when it comes to ensuring consumer and investor protection.
The aim here is to identify the needs of specific customer groups.
Not every small saver needs access to all highly complex documents.

The issue involves defining appropriate products for target groups, conducting product tests, telephone recordings, appropriate consultations, disclosure obligations, and, importantly, introducing subsidiarity in the area of commissions.
Member States should have the possibility, according to their respective structures, to make appropriate adjustments.
I think we are on the right track.
Once my time is up, I would like to thank everyone who has contributed here.
I find it regrettable that it is considered so difficult that everyone gets only four minutes of speaking time.
I would like to thank all my colleagues who have contributed here at the Commission with their very constructive role over the past weeks and months.
I hope that with a strong mandate from the plenary session tomorrow, we will be able to enter negotiations with the Council and, as soon as possible, conclude this important file. Thank you.
Dear colleagues! Thank you very much for the generally good cooperation on this report.
We have been able to further improve the Commission proposal, which was already a significant step in the right direction, at key locations.
In the area of market structures, there will be greater transparency, with more comprehensive transparency specifically in the area of derivatives trading.
Under the MiFID framework, our compromise agreements on high-frequency trading go significantly further than the Commission's original proposals and deserve the support of the Parliament as a whole.
And even on issues that have provoked significant public criticism, the report points in the right direction.

In the area of commodity and food speculation, following extensive and difficult debates—something that must also be acknowledged—the limits are now much more strictly enforced than the Commission's decisions, as position limits are now being established as binding.
However, we still need further changes in this area to close remaining gaps.
I also hope for further additions during the plenary vote and, ultimately, in the trilogue.
And it is encouraging that Mr. Barnier has already signaled his support for the Parliament's more far-reaching proposal.
Where the report, however, has weaknesses is in the area of consumer protection.
We now have the possibility to prohibit certain products. This is a step forward. However, we still have no changes in the area of commission-driven advice.
Indeed, the distribution of financial products is not primarily oriented toward the interests of customers, but rather strongly dominated by the extent of commissions available for each product.
In Germany in particular, but throughout the EU, consumers have lost a great deal of money.
Consider the open-ended real estate funds.
For citizens in countries such as France, Italy, Spain, and Germany, relatively little will change even after our compromise.
The fundamental principle will continue to be commission orientation.
Referencing subsidiarity here is a bit simplistic.
Here, I would have preferred stricter regulations.
Madam President, Commissioner, dear colleagues! Yes, what is worth waiting for in the end turns out to be good—I can only say that.
The report on the type approval of vehicles in the L category has ultimately turned out to be a good report after all.
The work took considerably longer overall than any of us had anticipated, let alone hoped. And there were far more setbacks than ultimately suited us.
But at this point, I would like to once again sincerely thank the rapporteur, Mr. Van der Kampf, for the truly cooperative and constructive collaboration.
Nevertheless, we have achieved a great deal together in the negotiations with the member states.
As rapporteur for my political group, I recommend approving this report. That said, it could have been much better.
To be better, my highly esteemed colleagues, I mean safer.
For us Social Democrats, the safety of vehicles—and thus the safety of drivers, particularly scooter riders and riders of motorcycles—has been a priority from the outset.
The number of motorcyclists involved in accidents has remained constant for years.
Generally, the number of accident fatalities in Europe is declining, except for motorcycle accidents, which remain a notable exception.
Reducing the number of motorcyclists and moped riders killed is indeed a matter close to my heart.
Fewer deaths on European roads—that was, and remains, my goal.
Every dead person is one too many.
This issue must be thoroughly addressed, particularly in a regulation so advantageous for the internal market.
That this can be achieved through effective technologies of anti-lock systems, namely ABS, remains something I am still convinced of.
And I am equally convinced of the cost-effectiveness.
It is simply not true that ABS is too expensive.

Nevertheless, we have achieved that from 2016 all nine motorcycle types and from 2017 all existing motorcycle types with an engine displacement of 125 cc and above must be equipped with ABS by the manufacturer in a binding manner.
With motorcycle models, manufacturers could optionally equip vehicles with either ABS or combined braking systems.
In this respect, we must unfortunately admit that we have not achieved any improvement over the Commission's proposal.
I would have wished for much more here. But the Commission and the Member States have, to put it mildly, shown themselves to be very sluggish.
Nevertheless, we have achieved that in 2019 the Commission presented a report.
The report must be based on traffic accident data from the Member States and should examine the mandatory installation of ABS on motorcycles.
Furthermore, it is sent to the Parliament and the Council.
On the basis of this report, the Commission will then consider making the installation of ABS mandatory for trailers.
You see, the topic remains very, very alive.
Therefore, we will endeavor to close the binding gap in legislation starting in 2019.
I am certain that the collected data will support my position.
I am convinced that this is indeed a good day for motorcycle riders across Europe and that accident rates will decrease in the future.
Mr. President, Matthias Groth, there is no technology without risks. There is no perfect energy generation technology.
And I believe this also applies to what we are discussing today.
I am strongly in favor of our discussing this in a rational manner.
What we are currently experiencing is a new influx into energy markets through unconventional resources, both in gas—predominantly in gas—and also to some extent in oil.

I believe it is neither appropriate to lapse into great euphoria about the great gas future. Equally inappropriate is what some of my colleagues from the Greens and the Left have done here—painting dire and frightening scenarios that have nothing at all to do with reality.

I believe we must keep in mind that these unconventional resources have the potential to transform all energy supply relationships we know—between suppliers and demanders—and that they also challenge the economic viability calculations of conventional energy carriers.
We must confront this development.

I believe we should recognize that this may also present an opportunity for Europe, and not merely a risk. We should prepare ourselves to positively embrace and utilize new technologies, to fairly weigh opportunities against risks, and avoid falling into ideological debates. Thank you.

have jointly decided. Then we must inform those who are responsible, those who are genuinely engaged in a European manner, as thoroughly and precisely as possible.

Mr. President, ladies and gentlemen! Five years ago, the EU's Central Asia strategy was launched.
When asking today for an assessment of the outcome of this strategy, the evaluation is ambivalent.

We see how the member states individually strive to establish good economic relations, particularly with the resource-rich countries in this region, while at the same time these efforts make no progress.
To accompany states on their transformation process toward modern democratic societies, or as the recent events in Kazakhstan show, development can even be marked by setbacks.

Nevertheless, precisely for this reason, the European Union and its member states must more strongly express their influence in this region together, particularly toward Kazakhstan, which has now become a major supplier for numerous EU countries.

Isolating Kazakhstan by the EU would merely enable competitors to exert even greater influence in this geopolitically significant country, where adherence to civil and human rights is less of a priority.
The integration of economic interests with the promotion of human rights is the central element of the Central Asia strategy.
This should also serve as the basis for further negotiations on a Partnership and Cooperation Agreement with Kazakhstan.
The present report provides good orientation on this matter. Many thanks.

Mr. President, ladies and gentlemen of the absent Council! Perhaps you are following us via the Parliament’s video stream, as you evidently have neither the capacity nor the will to speak with us live.
I regard the absence of the Council here and today as a gross disregard of Parliament.

Rather than avoiding this debate, you would be better advised to reveal your secret reason for opposing any improvement in the protection of personal data in the fields of police and justice through a directive.

For years, police and justice authorities have been, as we know in Eldorado, centers of data protection violations—not only in the case of SWIFT, but also, for example, on a daily basis, where data of citizens, such as those demonstrating against Nazis, are unlawfully collected and stored.
We need the same protection standards for the public and private sectors, as well as for police and justice. This is what it's about—ensuring the guarantee of civil rights.

If the Parliament demands that the Council actively contribute to data protection-relevant regulations such as EU, PNA, or Europol, then the same should presumably also apply to the Council in reverse.
Therefore, I urge you to put an end to the blocking policy in the field of data protection.
